answer:
**The following works in the corpus substantively engage with the rights or treatment of slaves, primarily through legal codes detailing types of slaves, their acquisition, duties, manumission conditions, punishments, inheritance rules, and protections (or lack thereof), or through philosophical/economic analysis of slavery's societal impacts, compatibility with liberty, and historical treatment.**[^1][^2][^3][^4][^5][^6][^7]

These are drawn exclusively from the Dharmashastras (ancient Indic legal texts) and 19th-century French political-economic treatises. Poetic works (e.g., *Bang-E-Dara*, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, *Diwan-E-Zauq*) use "slave" metaphorically (e.g., enslaved by love or passion) without legal or philosophical engagement on rights/treatment.[^8] No other works meet the criteria of substantive discussion.

### Indic Dharmashastras (legal treatment, origins, rights, duties, manumission)
- **Bṛhaspati Smṛti**: Details 19 types of slaves (e.g., born in house, purchased), work classifications (impure/pure), manumission (e.g., saving master's life), inheritance shares, and punishments.[^9]
- **Kātyāyana Smṛti** (esp. Chapter 63): Extensive rules on slavery's origins (voluntary surrender except for Brahmins), caste restrictions (no enslaving higher castes), manumission (e.g., pair of oxen, bearing master's child), property rights (master owns slave's wealth), marriage rules, and punishments for mistreatment.[^10]
- **Manusmṛti** (Chapters 8–10): Seven origins of slaves (e.g., captured, born in house), treatment (beating allowed for faults), property (belongs to master), offspring rules, and prohibitions (no enslaving Brahmins).[^11]
- **Nārada Smṛti**: 15–20 types of slaves/workers, hereditary vs. temporary slavery, manumission (e.g., famine relief via oxen, debt repayment), witness incompetence, and caste order (no reverse-order slavery).[^12]
- **Āṅgirasa Smṛti**: Minor but direct: equates slave labor with lowly occupations; implies treatment via occupational hierarchies.[^13]
- **Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana**: Brief cosmological reference to rebirth as slaves/servants for sins, implying degraded treatment.[^14]

### French Political-Economic Treatises (philosophical critique of treatment and rights)
- **Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I** (Charles Dunoyer, esp. Chapters 7–8): Analyzes slavery's historical treatment (e.g., Romans, Greeks, colonies), effects on masters/slaves (brutalization, population decline), racial aspects, manumission challenges, and incompatibility with liberty.[^15]

**Charles Comte's *Traité de Législation* series discusses slavery's moral/legal effects across volumes (e.g., Vol. I critiques climate justifications, Vol. II racial hierarchies enabling enslavement, Vol. III historical enslavement patterns), portraying it as corrupting treatment that brutalizes societies.**[^16]

No other works (of 37 total) discuss slaves beyond metaphor or isolated references.

[^1]: Search results aggregate all "slave/slaves/slavery/enslaved" hits, filtered for substantive legal/philosophical content.
[^2]: *Kātyāyana Smṛti*, Chapter 63, [p-fbeb35](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2/63/translation/#p-fbeb35).
[^3]: *Manusmṛti*, Chapter 8, [p-5be08b](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/08/translation/#p-5be08b).
[^4]: *Nāradasmṛti*, Chapter 1, [p-b5b2f4](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-naradasmrti-7650d2/01/translation/#p-b5b2f4).
[^5]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, Chapter 1, [p-0d0bc4](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-0d0bc4).
[^6]: *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, Chapter 7, [p-03bfac](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/07-ii-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des/translation/#p-03bfac).
[^7]: *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, Chapter 2, [p-56a86f](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/02-de-la-methode-analytique-appliquee-a-l-etude-de-la/translation/#p-56a86f).
[^8]: E.g., *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [p-6bee14](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/04-dil-se-jo-baat-nikalti-hai-asar-rakhti-hai/translation/#p-6bee14) (metaphorical "slavery’s cruel chains").
[^9]: *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, Chapter 1, [p-0d0bc4](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-0d0bc4).
[^10]: *Kātyāyana Smṛti*, Chapter 63, [p-fbeb35](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-katyayana-smrti-1e06d2/63/translation/#p-fbeb35).
[^11]: *Manusmṛti*, Chapter 8, [p-5be08b](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/08/translation/#p-5be08b).
[^12]: *Nāradasmṛti*, Chapter 1, [p-ba7fe2](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-naradasmrti-7650d2/01/translation/#p-ba7fe2).
[^13]: *Āṅgirasa Smṛti*, Chapter 1, [p-73c279](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-73c279).
[^14]: *Kunjarakarna Dharmakathana*, Chapter 1, [p-8f2ce2](https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-kunjarakarna-dharmakathana-894f4a/01/translation/#p-8f2ce2).
[^15]: *Nouveau traité d'économie: VOL I*, Chapter 7, [p-03bfac](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-dunoyer-nouveau-traite-deconomie-vol-i-6da8ce/07-ii-liberte-compatible-avec-la-maniere-de-vivre-des/translation/#p-03bfac).
[^16]: *Traité de Législation: VOL I*, Chapter 3, [p-16cb4b](https://falsafa.corpus/works/charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2/03-de-l-influence-qu-exerce-sur-les-moeurs-et-sur-les/translation/#p-16cb4b).
